finding.summary: The captured region is 78% silence, so every level/LUFS statistic is heavily diluted by dead air. In the remaining ~22% of the capture the synth bass reaches a sample peak of -18.0 dBFS, an RMS of -42.0 dBFS, a crest factor of 12.0 dB, integrated LUFS of -20.0, and an LRA of 8.0 LU. The 1/3-octave snapshot shows the expected bass tilt (100 Hz at -18.0 dB, 1 kHz at -24.0 dB, 4 kHz at -30.0 dB). The track has no FX chain loaded, no sends, no receives, no parent bus, phase not inverted, panned center, fader at 0.0 dB, trim/read automation.
finding.probable_cause: The material itself is sparse — sustained synth bass notes with long rests between phrases is typical for this kind of part — and/or the captured region includes pre-roll or a non-playing section. With 78% silence, the RMS, integrated LUFS, and LRA reflect mostly the noise floor between notes rather than the bass notes themselves, so a 'quiet' reading here is not necessarily a gain-staging problem.
finding.confidence_reason: Confidence is capped at low because audio.silence_fraction is 0.780.

evidence_refs.description: 0.78 — ≥ 0.75 threshold that gates confidence and prevents any actionable proposal
-18.0 dB at 100 Hz — expected bass weight

proposal.reason: audio.silence_fraction is 0.78 (≥ 0.75), so per protocol no actionable move can be supported from these diluted level statistics. The low RMS/LUFS readings may simply reflect the part's rest-heavy performance rather than a gain-staging problem, and the tonal balance (100 Hz dominant, falling -6 dB to 1 kHz and -6 dB again to 4 kHz) looks bass-appropriate. Without a capture of the active passages, raising the fader, adding an EQ, or bus-routing the track would all be guesses. Recommend re-capturing a region that actually contains the bass lines, then re-running the check.
